$description: How each elevation level is drawn. Stemcell's, not a theme's and not a consumer's: the constitution names Elevation a structural layer and allows overriding only colour and material (Art.3), and a shadow's offsets and blur are quantity, which Art.7 counts as normative. So this file is not a theme file — nothing a consumer authors reaches it — but it is built into every theme, once per theme, and resolves against that theme's colours there. That last part is not decoration: a custom property holding var() is resolved where it is declared, so an elevation declared once at :root would hand every subtree the root theme's shadow. Declared per theme, [data-theme] works at any scope, which is what Art.6 means by a scope attribute. The only thing that changes between themes is colour — the surfaces below, and color.app.shadow-umbra / -penumbra.

surface.$description: The ground plane takes the app background.

shadow.$description: Flat sits on the ground plane and casts nothing, in every theme.

umbra (tight, dark) over penumbra (wide, soft), lit from directly above. The two-layer geometry is this platform's expression of the level, not the level itself — a platform that can only draw one shadow keeps the level and renders one (Art.7).

surface.$description: App bar / sidebar / sticky header / tab bar. Shares a surface with level 1; they do not overlap, so the shadow carries the difference.
Menu / dropdown / select / combobox / popover. Tooltip draws at this depth but stacks at --layer-tooltip.

Toast / snackbar. Shares the overlay surface with modal, so in dark — where the tonal ladder has run out of headroom before it runs out of levels — the shadow is the only thing separating a toast from the dialog beneath it.
